statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph with V vertices and E edges, and an integer M, determine if it is possible to color the graph using M colors such that no two adjacent vertices have the same color.

constraints.description: Number of vertices in the graph
Number of edges in the graph

examples.explanation: It is possible to color the graph with 3 colors
It is not possible to color the graph with 2 colors

hints.text: To solve this problem, you should first understand the basics of graph theory, including how to represent a graph using an adjacency list or matrix, and how to traverse a graph using depth-first search (DFS) or breadth-first search (BFS).

The M Coloring Problem is a classic problem in graph theory that can be solved using a backtracking algorithm. You should consider how to assign colors to each vertex in the graph, and how to check if the current color assignment is valid by ensuring that no two adjacent vertices have the same color.

A key insight to solving this problem is to use a recursive function that attempts to assign a color to each vertex in the graph, one at a time. If a valid color assignment is found, the function should return true. If no valid color assignment is found after trying all possible colors, the function should return false, indicating that it is not possible to color the graph using M colors.

editorial.title: M Coloring Problem
editorial.intuition: The M Coloring Problem is an NP-complete problem that involves assigning colors to the vertices of a graph such that no two adjacent vertices have the same color. The problem can be solved using a brute force approach by trying all possible color combinations, but this approach is inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach tries all possible color combinations for the vertices of the graph. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(m^v), where m is the number of colors available and v is the number of vertices in the graph.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution can be achieved by using a backtracking algorithm, which tries different color combinations and backtracks when a conflict is found. This approach is still not optimal but is more efficient than the brute force approach.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a backtracking algorithm with a safety check to ensure that a vertex can be assigned a color without conflicting with its adjacent vertices. This approach has a time complexity of O(m^v) and a space complexity of O(v), where m is the number of colors available and v is the number of vertices in the graph.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the optimal solution involves showing that the algorithm tries all possible color combinations and that the safety check ensures that no two adjacent vertices have the same color. The algorithm backtracks when a conflict is found, ensuring that all possible solutions are explored.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm involves trying different color combinations and checking for conflicts without actually assigning colors to the vertices. This can help to identify potential issues with the algorithm and ensure that it is working correctly.
commonMistakes: Not checking for conflicts between adjacent vertices
Not backtracking when a conflict is found
Not trying all possible color combinations
Not using a safety check to ensure that a vertex can be assigned a color without conflicting with its adjacent vertices
Not handling the base case correctly in the recursive function

functionSignature.java: public boolean graphColoring(int[][] graph, int m);
